rationale: This page provides detailed information on Centre-State relations in India, including the federal scheme, constitutional provisions (Articles 246, 248-254), and the procedure for the reorganisation of states under Article 3. It also lists key initiatives like the Inter-State Council and GST Council, making it highly suitable for generating polity-related questions.
cleanedText:
6. CENTRE-STATE RELATIONS
6.1. STATES’ DEMAND FOR AUTONOMY
Why in the News?
Tamil Nadu government has formed a high-level committee, headed by former Supreme Court judge Justice Kurian Joseph, to suggest ways to strengthen State autonomy and federalism.
Federal Scheme of Indian Constitution
* India is a union of states, with no right to secede.
* It has common features such as single constitution, single citizenship, all-India services, election commission and single unified judiciary.
* Division of Legislative Powers
* Article 246: Distributes powers between Parliament and States through Seventh Schedule.
* Union list, State list; Concurrent list.
* Any constitutional amendment affecting Lists in Seventh Schedule must be ratified by at least half of the State legislatures.
* Indian federalism is quasi-federal with a stronger Centre and gives it more power than States.
Constitutional Provisions related to Federalism
* Article 248: Residuary Powers rest with Parliament
* Article 249: Laws on State subjects for national interest
* Article 250: Centre can legislate on state list items during emergencies.
* Article 252: Parliament makes laws for States with their consent.
* Article 253: International treaties override State powers.
* Article 254: Union law prevails in case of conflict.
Centre-State Relations Initiatives
* Inter-State Council: Constitutional body under Article 263 to facilitate Centre and State coordination.
* Zonal Councils: Statutory bodies set up under the States Reorganisation Act, 1956.
* NITI Aayog: Replaces the Planning Commission, promoting cooperative federalism with more state participation.
* GST Council: Constitutional body under Article 279A for joint Centre-State decision-making on GST policies.
* Decentralisation: 73rd & 74th Amendments empowered Panchayats and Municipalities as a third tier of government.
6.1.1. REORGANISATION OF STATE
Recently, Uttarakhand marked 25 years of its formation.
Procedure for Formation of New States
* Article 3: Provisions for formation of new States and alteration of areas, boundaries or names of existing States. As per this,
* Power: Parliament may by law form a new State.
* Presidential Recommendation: Such Bill shall be introduced in either House of Parliament only on President’s recommendation.
* Consultation with State Legislatures: Before recommending a Bill, The President shall refer this Bill to Legislature of that State to express its view within a specified time.
* Parliament can create a new state through ordinary legislation (i.e. with simple majority).
